classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Auburn Building and Loan Association.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Auburn Building and Loan Association, outlining its corporators, name, corporate powers, stock structure, officers, and operational commencement. It grants the association perpetual succession, the ability to sue and be sued, contract, hold property, issue stock up to $50,000, and loan funds at legal interest rates.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act of incorporation for a building and loan association. It details the organizational structure, powers, and financial mechanisms of the association. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Monticello, and to regulate the traffic in spirituous liquors therein.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the judge of Wayne county court or the board of trustees of Monticello from granting licenses to sell spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors within the town limits or within one mile of the court-house, effective April 1, 1873. Exceptions are made for druggists and apothecaries who can be licensed to sell for medicinal purposes only, provided they obtain a written order from a practicing physician or surgeon, specifying the buyer, quantity, date, and medicinal purpose. The order is valid for one sale only. The act also specifies that spirits sold under these exceptions must be removed within twenty-four hours.
classification.reasoning: The statute regulates the sale of spirituous liquors in Monticello, Wayne County. It outlines licensing procedures and exceptions for druggists selling for medicinal purposes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and does not suggest any racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to regulate the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors, and mixtures thereof, in less quantities than ten gallons."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regulating the sale of alcoholic beverages in quantities less than ten gallons. It clarifies what constitutes a retailer, mandates immediate removal of purchased liquors from the premises, and outlines penalties for violations. It also specifies fees for prosecuting attorneys, the issuance of capias pro fines, and liability for physicians who provide orders in evasion of the act's spirit. Jurisdiction for violations is conferred on specific town and county officials, as well as the circuit court of Wayne County. Prosecutions are to be in the name of the board of trustees of the town of Monticello.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details regulations on the sale of alcoholic beverages, including quantity restrictions, delivery requirements, penalties for violations, and jurisdictional matters. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The act appears to be a general regulation of liquor sales. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1873'.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Masonic Hall Association of Louisville
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Masonic Hall Association of Louisville, outlining provisions for leasing rooms, issuing and transferring stock, declaring dividends, coercing payment of stock subscriptions, allowing the association to borrow money and issue certificates for it, and establishing a registry for issued certificates. classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to the town of Shelbyville.
classification.summary: This act consolidates and amends previous laws concerning the town of Shelbyville. It defines the town's boundaries, vests its municipal concerns in seven trustees elected annually by free male inhabitants over 21 who have resided in the town for six months. Trustees serve one-year terms, must take an oath of office, and are empowered to fill vacancies. Qualifications for trustees include being a citizen and resident for at least twelve months prior to election. The trustees are established as a corporate body known as 'The Board of Trustees of the Town of Shelbyville'.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal governance of the town of Shelbyville, specifically outlining the election, qualifications, and powers of its trustees. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The voting qualifications are based on gender ('free male inhabitants') and residency, which, while discriminatory by modern standards, do not specifically target or segregate based on race in the context of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

Additionally, it establishes penalties for damaging public trees, shrubs, and fencing.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the town of Smithland" approved March 10, 1860, and to confer additional powers upon the police judge of said town.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and jurisdiction of a police judge in the town of Smithland, Kentucky. It details their authority over misdemeanors, civil and criminal cases within the scope of justices of the peace, examining trials, bail recognizances, and specific offenses such as affrays, assaults, riots, drunkenness, and other conduct deemed disruptive to the town's peace. It also grants the judge exclusive jurisdiction over offenses related to town ordinances and cases where the punishment does not exceed a hundred-dollar fine. The judge can summon juries, issue witness summons, and punish contempt of court with a fine not exceeding ten dollars or imprisonment for twelve hours. Prosecutions are to be initiated by summons or warrant.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the judicial powers of a police judge within a specific town. It defines the types of cases the judge can hear, the penalties they can impose, and the procedures they must follow. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on the administrative and judicial functions of a local official. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The company must create a plat of the land, divide it into lots, and sell burial lots as prescribed by its board of directors.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 94 of the General Statutes, Title Roads and Turnpikes, So Far as it Relates to the County of Hardin.
classification.summary: This act concerns the administration and funding of roads in Hardin County, Kentucky. It proposes a referendum to determine whether roads should be maintained by taxation instead of by mandatory labor (road workings). If approved by a majority vote, taxation would become the method of road maintenance, and existing laws requiring road workings would be repealed for Hardin County. The act also states that if it applies to Hardin County, it shall also apply to Meade County. It details the duties and responsibilities of road superintendents and their deputies, including bonding, accountability, and reporting requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the management and funding of roads in Hardin and Meade counties. It introduces a vote on whether to switch from a system of mandatory labor ('road workings') to a system funded by taxation for road maintenance. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, nor does it establish any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1873. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about road administration and not racial segregation.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 65

Farley, and their associates and successors, be, and they are hereby, constituted a body politic under the name and style of the Mechanics' Loan and Building Association, of Lexington, Kentucky, by which name they shall have succession, and may sue and be sued, implead and be impleaded, contract and be contracted with; may have a common seal, and break or alter the same at pleasure; may acquire and hold real and personal estate, and sell, mortgage, or otherwise dispose of the same.

**1873.**
*Name and style.*

*Corporate powers.*

§ 2. The officers of the Association shall be a president, vice president, solicitor, secretary, treasurer, and twelve directors, who, after the first election, shall be elected on the first Thursday in January of each year, and shall continue in office until their successors are elected and qualified. If for any reason the officers are not elected on the day fixed above, they may be elected at a meeting of the association called for that purpose; notice of which meeting, and the object thereof, shall be given by publication, for at least two weeks, in three or more newspapers published in the city of Lexington; and it shall be the duty of the board of directors to call a meeting for the purpose of electing officers, within one month after the day fixed for the regular election.

*Officers—when elected and terms of office.*

§ 3. The president, vice president, and directors shall constitute a board of directors for the management of the business of the association. The board of directors shall have power to remove all officers, except members of the board, and to fill vacancies in all offices; and the person elected to fill a vacancy shall serve during the remainder of the term, and until his successor shall be elected and qualified. The association may require any officer to give bond for the faithful performance of his duties.

*Powers and duties of directors.*

§ 4. The capital stock of the association shall be five hundred thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of two hundred dollars each, and to be paid by the stockholders in weekly installments, of fifty cents each week on each share, for five years from the first election of officers; unless before that time said installments, with their net accumulations, shall amount to two hundred dollars for each share; in which case, and at all events at the end of said five years, there shall be a distribution of the cash assets on hand, and a liquidation of the affairs of the association; and after that time its corporate powers and existence shall only continue for winding up its business: *Provided*, That if a portion of the members desire to continue the existence of the association, they may do so for an additional period, the same as provided for its original existence. The members retiring must be paid

*Capital stock.*

VOL. II—LOC. L.—5

The rate of toll is to be in accordance with the length of the road used. ## CHAPTER 531.
AN ACT in relation to the assessment and collection of turnpike taxes in Lewis county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That all tax-payers liable to taxation on any turnpike road in Lewis county shall have the right, on or before November first of each year, to pay their taxes to the treasurer of the company; and if not paid on or before that day, the same shall be listed with the sheriff for collection, and the sheriff shall collect said tax, and ten per cent. in addition, which ten per cent. shall be retained by the sheriff in full for his fees for collecting.

§ 2. The turnpike companies of said county shall have the right to appoint their own assessors, and allow them not exceeding ten cents for each list returned to be paid out of the taxes collected.

§ 3. This act shall take effect from its passage.
Approved March 22, 1873.

---

## CHAPTER 532.
AN ACT in relation to granting tavern license in the Vanceburg election precinct, in Lewis county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That hereafter the Lewis county court shall not grant a license to a tavern-keeper to sell spirituous liquors by the small, in the Vanceburg election precinct, in said county, outside of the limits of the city of Vanceburg, until said tavern-keeper shall have paid to the trustees of the Vanceburg Male and Female Academy the sum of not less than fifty ($50) nor more than one hundred dollars ($100), the amount to be fixed by said trustees, and shall produce in court the receipt of the trustees for the amount so paid; and which money the said trustees shall use in erecting the building for the school-house and male and female academy; and when the same shall be paid for, then the money so collected shall be used in teaching the free school in said male and female academy.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Crab Orchard. It specifies that six jurors are sufficient for trials of breaches of the peace, riots, and other violations of town ordinances. It also restricts the town trustees' power to tax farm lands within the new boundary, allowing taxation only on dwelling-houses with one acre of land and lots laid off for building purposes. Finally, it sets the schedule for the police judge's court and allows the town clerk to act as the clerk for the police court.

It grants the company perpetual succession and empowers it to contract, own property, and engage in various industrial activities including coal mining, building blast furnaces, machine shops, rolling mills, nail factories, and manufacturing wood, clay, or iron products. It also allows the company to build railroads from its lands to the Ohio River for transporting minerals and manufactured goods.

It details the roles of city officials like the marshal, treasurer, and clerk, and grants the city council powers to levy taxes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not considered a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Paducah
classification.summary: This act grants the city council of Paducah broad powers to regulate businesses, including the ability to levy taxes on various establishments such as taverns, coffee-houses, billiard tables, circuses, menageries, and exhibitions. It also empowers the council to grade, pave, and repair streets, abate nuisances, establish a police system, regulate burying grounds, and establish markets. The council can fix license fees and revoke licenses for violations of regulations. Penalties are prescribed for violations of the section.
classification.reasoning: The statute grants general municipal powers to the city council of Paducah. It details the council's authority to tax and regulate various businesses and public services. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1873).

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This act details various powers granted to the city council of Louisville, including the ability to license and regulate vehicles for hire, suppress gaming houses and houses of ill-fame, and punish disorderly conduct. It also outlines the appropriation of city funds, the posting of financial statements, and the handling of fines and forfeitures. Notably, it allows the city to use the Graves county jail for the confinement of convicted individuals.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is on general municipal governance and law enforcement. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The county court can enlarge these duties and set the commissioner's compensation and require a bond.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of common schooldistrictNo. 24 , in Larue county.
classification.summary: This act allows for a vote within common school district No. 24 in Larue County to determine if a tax should be imposed on taxable property owned by white persons in the district. classification.summary: This act establishes a common school district named Urania district, specifying that qualified white persons residing within the district are permitted to vote for its trustees. It outlines the election process for trustees, their term of office, oath requirements, and responsibilities, including reporting the number of children within the district and receiving school funds. The act also details how vacancies in the trustee office are to be filled.

Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the racial indicator is explicit. The category is 'education' as it pertains to school districts and trustees. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to provide for the common schools of Kentucky," approved March 23, 1870, and to provide for the levy of a tax for common schools.
classification.summary: This act allows school district trustees to submit a proposition to white voters and property owners for a tax levy to benefit common schools. It outlines the voting process, notice requirements, and the duties of election officials. If the tax is approved by a majority of qualified voters, the trustees are empowered to levy and collect it based on the commissioner's book assessment.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the funding of common schools through local taxation. While it mentions 'white children' and 'black children' in the context of separate enumeration (which could be a precursor to segregation), the core of the statute is about the mechanism for levying taxes for schools. The voting process is described as being for 'white voters' and 'others in the county having property therein'. This explicit mention of 'white voters' for a school tax proposition, in the context of the era, strongly suggests a racially discriminatory voting process for school funding. However, the statute itself does not mandate segregation or create separate facilities. It focuses on the financial aspect of education. Given the explicit mention of 'white voters' in the context of a school tax, it is not a direct Jim Crow law in the sense of segregation or disenfranchisement, but it operates within a system where racial distinctions were made. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for being a Jim Crow law, but the confidence is high because the racial element is present in the voting eligibility. The category is 'education' as it directly relates to school funding. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V2-1873.pdf' and the format of the text. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'white voters'. Human review is not needed as the classification is clear.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the common schools of the county of Barren,' approved March 11, 1870, so far as it relates to the district of Glasgow.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning common schools in Barren County, specifically for the district of Glasgow. It outlines how school funds should be applied, including the possibility of securing tuition from Urania College and Allen Lodge Female College. It also details procedures for local taxation for schools, including voter notification, tax duration, and the submission of a proposition for a two-dollar per capita tax on each white male citizen aged twenty-one and older. The chairman of the trustees is required to provide a bond before receiving funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white male citizens' in relation to a per capita tax for school purposes. While it does not establish separate schools or explicitly discriminate in the provision of education, the explicit mention of race in a taxation context for public services warrants careful consideration. However, the primary focus of the statute is on the funding and administration of common schools, and the racial designation appears in the context of who is subject to a specific tax, not in the segregation or denial of educational services. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence, as it doesn't fit the typical definition of a Jim Crow law that enforces segregation or disenfranchisement. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the legislature in 1873. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the direct mention of 'white male citizens'.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the establishment of graded schools in the county of Barren,' approved March 11, 1870.
classification.summary: This act details the financial and administrative procedures for trustees of graded schools in Barren County, Kentucky. It outlines the bonding requirements for the chairman, their daily fees, the clerk's fees for elections and record-keeping, and the chairman's commission on funds handled. It also mandates the keeping of detailed records, open for public inspection, and specifies penalties for violations by trustees or unqualified voters, and for the destruction of records. The act focuses on the operational aspects of school management and does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administration and funding of graded schools in Barren County, Kentucky. It details the roles and responsibilities of trustees, the chairman, and the clerk, as well as financial matters like fees and record-keeping. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature. The confidence is high because the absence of racial indicators is clear.

classification.title: AN ACT to establish a separate school for white children in a district in Barren county.
classification.summary: This act establishes a separate school for white children in a specific district in Barren County. It explicitly states that no tax will be collected from colored persons or their property for this school, and colored children will not have access to its benefits. The act also outlines the process for its ratification by qualified white voters.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the school is for 'white children' and that 'no tax shall be collected off of any colored person or his property' for this school, nor will 'any colored child have the benefits of the schools herein provided'. This clearly indicates a racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1873. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. No human review is needed.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Evansville and Henderson Railroad Packet Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Evansville and Henderson Railroad Packet Company, granting it the power to own or hire vessels, transport railroad cars, passengers, and freight between Evansville, Indiana, and Henderson, Kentucky, and other points. It also allows the company to connect with railroad tracks in these cities and operate its own tracks with municipal consent. The company's capital stock is capped at $100,000.

classification.title: AN ACT amending an act incorporating the Vanceburg, Salt Lick, Tollsboro, and Maysville Turnpike Road Company.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Vanceburg, Salt Lick, Tollsboro, and Maysville Turnpike Road Company. It authorizes the county court of Lewis County to subscribe $2,000 to the company's stock for building bridges over Salt Lick Creek and Cabin Creek. The county judge is empowered to issue county bonds for this subscription, drawing 10% interest. The act also grants the road company directors the right to appoint assessors to value taxable property for road construction, with the Lewis County Court having oversight over these assessments. Assessor books are to be returned before May 1st annually.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about infrastructure and finance, with no racial implications.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the school commissioner of Campbell county to redistrict the school districts of said county, and to repeal an act, entitled " An act to provide for the redistricting the school districts of Campbell county," approved March 5, 1872.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the school commissioner of Campbell County to redistrict school districts, define their boundaries, and make a record of them. It includes provisions to ensure that schoolhouses remain in a central location within their districts and that districts are only altered according to the general school law. The act also repeals a previous act from March 5, 1872, concerning redistricting school districts in Campbell County. The work is to be completed by September 1, 1873, with compensation to be determined by the court of claims. Schools will operate under the current district arrangements for the school year ending June 30, 1874.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative process of redistricting school districts within Campbell County, Kentucky. classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the county court of Anderson county to issue bonds for the relief of sufferers by fire.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Anderson County to issue bonds to provide relief for those affected by a fire in Lawrenceburg on March 15, 1873. The court can appoint commissioners to sell the bonds and make arrangements for interest and principal payments. The proceeds are to be used for the relief of fire sufferers, with discretion given to the court or its commissioners regarding the beneficiaries' merit. Before issuing bonds or levying taxes, the question must be submitted to a vote of qualified voters in Anderson County.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the issuance of bonds by Anderson County to provide relief to victims of a fire. It outlines the process for bond issuance, taxation, and voter approval. There is no mention of race, and the relief is for all sufferers of the fire, with discretion based on merit. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's purpose is clearly defined and lacks any racial component.

Calhoun is unable to collect these debts himself due to his physical condition, and other collecting officers have refused to handle them. The appointed collector will have the powers of a sheriff in levying, attaching, and selling property for collection, and must execute a bond to the county court. The bonds will bear interest up to 10% annually, be issued in denominations not exceeding $1,000, and mature within 15 years, redeemable after two years. The created fund will be managed separately by the board of trustees.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute, Chapter 649, concerns the establishment and funding of school buildings in a specific district in Hawesville. It details the process of defining district boundaries, issuing bonds for construction, and managing the funds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high as the content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in Crittenden county.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in Crittenden County. It makes it unlawful for county courts or town trustees to grant licenses for such sales. Selling or giving liquor to be drunk on the premises, except to a family member, is a punishable offense. The act's enforcement is contingent on a public vote on 'License, or no license' in August 1873, with provisions for resubmission if rejected.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to regulate the sale of ardent spirits and intoxicating beverages'
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation regarding the sale of alcoholic beverages. It prohibits the consumption of alcohol sold in quantities of one gallon or less than ten gallons on the premises of the vendor or premises under their control. If this prohibition is violated, the vendor is guilty of keeping a tippling-house and subject to penalties. Additionally, the vendor is liable for civil damages resulting from the use of the alcohol, with a right of action prioritized for the wife, then minor children, then any other person. The act also outlines the process for a public vote on its adoption.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of alcohol sales and consumption, specifically prohibiting drinking on the seller's premises for certain quantities. It also establishes a civil liability for damages resulting from alcohol use and outlines a public referendum process for the act's adoption. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the legislature in 1873. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about alcohol regulation and not racial segregation.

It defines the district boundaries and outlines the election of five trustees by qualified colored voters and widows with school-aged children. These trustees have exclusive control over the school, can sue and be sued, and are empowered to assess an annual poll tax of up to two dollars on each colored male over eighteen and widows with school-aged children within the district to fund the school.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the establishment of a colored school in Catlettsburg and Boyd county.
classification.summary: This act establishes a separate school for colored persons in Catlettsburg and Boyd County. It mandates that all municipal fines and penalties inflicted on colored persons for offenses committed in the district shall benefit this school. The delinquent tax list of colored persons will be turned over to the trustees for collection for the benefit of the colored school. The act also outlines the eligibility of scholars, qualifications of teachers, property acquisition for the schoolhouse, and fees for the treasurer and collector.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly establishes a separate school for 'colored persons' and dedicates fines, penalties, and taxes derived from this population to the benefit of this segregated school. This is a clear indicator of Jim Crow-era racial segregation in education. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1873. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of 'colored persons' and the establishment of separate facilities based on race. The category is 'education' as the statute's primary purpose is the establishment and funding of a school.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the Hickman county court to issue bonds for bridges, and to raise a sinking fund to pay same.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Hickman county court to issue bonds up to $8,000 for bridge construction, with a maximum ten-year term and a ten percent annual interest rate. It also establishes a sinking fund to manage the repayment of these bonds and associated expenses. The county court is mandated to levy an annual ad valorem tax and poll tax sufficient to cover county expenses, bond interest, and contribute at least $800 annually to the sinking fund until the bonds are fully liquidated. Three individuals are appointed as sinking fund commissioners, who will manage and loan out the collected funds, taking bonds and security. They are required to execute a bond to the Commonwealth of Kentucky, approved by the county court, double the amount of funds under their control, and this bond must be renewed annually. The commissioners are liable for malfeasance, misfeasance, or non-feasance of duty.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of Hickman County, specifically authorizing the issuance of bonds for bridge construction and the establishment of a sinking fund for repayment. It details the process of bond issuance, tax levies, and the duties and liabilities of appointed sinking fund commissioners. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The trustees are tasked with making a survey and plat of the town, which will be filed in the county clerk's office. The clerk is empowered to issue executions based on judgments from the quarterly court and to create transcripts of these records.

It defines the town's boundaries, appoints initial trustees, and establishes procedures for electing trustees and a police judge/town marshal. It also outlines the powers of the trustees, including the ability to pass ordinances and levy taxes for town expenses and improvements.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation of a town named Hanson, Kentucky. It outlines the administrative structure, election processes for town officials, and the powers of the town trustees, including taxation and ordinance creation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 219
classification.summary: This statute outlines the administrative and judicial functions of a town, including the appointment and duties of trustees, clerk, assessor, treasurer, police judge, and marshal. It details the process for property assessment, tax collection, and the jurisdiction of the police judge. It also specifies the bonding requirements for town officials and the procedures for filling vacancies.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the organizational structure and operational procedures for a town's government, including the roles and responsibilities of various officials like trustees, clerk, assessor, treasurer, police judge, and marshal. It covers aspects such as oath-taking, record-keeping, property assessment, tax collection, judicial jurisdiction, and financial management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1873) and page number, typical of legislative acts passed during a session.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the Winchester Cemetery Company.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Winchester Cemetery Company to assess and collect an annual tax from lot owners for cemetery maintenance and improvements. It also grants the company the power to enforce collection through warrants in the police court of Winchester or similar courts in other counties.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts. The first act (pages 252-253) concerns the fees and powers of a town marshal and the taxation of liquor sellers in a town within Hopkins County. The second act (Chapter 669, page 253) amends the charter of the Winchester Cemetery Company, granting it the power to levy taxes on lot owners for maintenance and to enforce collection. Neither of these acts explicitly or implicitly mentions race or racial segregation. They deal with municipal governance, taxation, and private company operations. Therefore, they are not considered Jim Crow laws.

classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of the Concord and Tollsboro Turnpike Road Company. It allows the company to issue bonds, erect toll gates, and collect tolls under specific conditions. It also permits the Lewis county court to subscribe for stock to aid in bridge construction and outlines provisions for charging extra tolls for heavy hauling and for damage to the road. The company can also appoint an assessor for property liable to taxation on the road.

classification.title: An Act to Establish a Board of Trustees for Winchester Public Schools
classification.summary: This act establishes a five-member Board of Trustees for the public schools of Winchester, Kentucky. It outlines their qualifications, oath of office, election of officers (president, clerk, treasurer), terms of office, and the process for filling vacancies. The board is granted control and management of the public schools, property, and funds, including the district's pro rata share of the common school fund. They are required to report to the county commissioner of common schools and publish an annual statement of receipts and expenditures. The board also has the power to make rules and by-laws for the government of themselves, their appointees, and the schools, including teachers and pupils. A majority constitutes a quorum, and meetings are scheduled monthly.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the establishment and governance of a public school board in Winchester, Kentucky. It outlines the administrative and financial responsibilities of the board, including the management of school property and funds, and the power to create rules for the schools. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from '1873', indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session. The confidence is high because the text is clear and lacks any racial indicators or discriminatory provisions.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Winchester, and to Provide for the Establishment of Common Schools Therein.
classification.summary: This act establishes a school system for the town of Winchester. It mandates separate schools for white and colored children, with distinct voting places and officers for each racial group during a tax levy election. The tax proceeds are to be appropriated solely to the respective racial schools based on the majority vote of each group.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates separate schools for 'white children' and 'children of color'. Furthermore, it details a voting process for a school tax that requires 'two voting places and two separate sets of officers, one for white voters and the other for negro voters.' This clear segregation in educational facilities and the electoral process for funding them is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial distinctions made in the text.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Winchester
classification.summary: This act allows the town of Winchester to levy taxes for public schools, contingent on a voter-approved proposition. Crucially, it explicitly states that white and colored children cannot attend the same school. It also mandates separate tax lists for whites and negroes, separate accounts for white and negro school funds, and specifies that bond proceeds are for white children's facilities. The election for school trustees is open to all persons qualified to vote for common schools.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates the segregation of children by race in schools ('nothing in this act shall be so construed as to allow white and colored children to attend the same school'). It also details separate financial accounting for 'White School Fund' and 'Negro School Fund', and specifies that funds are for 'white children'. This clearly falls under Jim Crow legislation related to education. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1873'.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the levy of an ad valorem tax for the purpose of building a school-house in school district No. 12, in Allen county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the levy of an ad valorem tax in school district No. 12, Allen County, for the purpose of building a school-house. The tax would be levied annually until a sufficient sum is raised, not exceeding one thousand dollars. Crucially, the tax could only be levied if approved by a majority of legally qualified white voters in the district. The collected funds were to be managed by trustees who would execute a bond and oversee the construction.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the tax for building a school-house shall only be levied if a majority of 'legally qualified white voters' in the district approve it. This is a clear instance of racial discrimination in the context of public services, specifically education, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. While it doesn't explicitly segregate students, it restricts the democratic process of taxation for public goods based on race. Therefore, it is classified as Jim Crow with an implicit racial indicator because the restriction is based on the racial identity of the voters, not on explicit racial segregation within the school itself. The confidence is high because the language is direct regarding the voting requirement.

classification.title: AN ACT to establish a colored free school at Bowling Green
classification.summary: This act authorizes the mayor and council of Bowling Green to levy a special tax on males of African descent over 21 and on property owned by persons of African descent to fund a free school for colored children aged six to twenty. It also establishes a board of trustees to manage these funds and oversee the school's establishment and support. Additionally, it directs the county judge to ascertain the city's share of a previously collected 'negro fund'.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored free school' and levies taxes specifically on 'persons of African descent' and 'negroes'. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The purpose is to establish a separate educational facility for Black children, funded by taxes levied on the Black community. The reference to a 'negro fund' further solidifies the racial classification. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and 'CHAPTER 696'. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language and segregationist intent.

classification.title: An Act for the benefit of the city of Bowling Green
classification.summary: This act allows the city of Bowling Green to levy a tax for the benefit of the city, with the condition that the tax can only be assessed if a majority of the colored voters in the city vote in favor of it. It also repeals previous general laws imposing taxes on persons of African descent in the city.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored voters', 'negroes', and 'mulattoes' in relation to taxation. While it frames the tax as being for the 'benefit of the city' and requires a vote from 'colored voters' to be implemented, the explicit mention of racial groups in the context of taxation and the repeal of prior taxes specifically on 'persons of African descent' strongly indicates a racial dimension. The mechanism of requiring a vote from a specific racial group to impose a tax, even if framed as a benefit, is a characteristic of discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is classified as Jim Crow. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The category is 'other' as it pertains to taxation and local governance rather than a specific area like education or housing.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the South Kentucky Fair Ground Association
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the South Kentucky Fair Ground Association. It specifies that officers will be elected on the first Saturday in November annually. It also makes it unlawful to sell, give, or furnish spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors within one-fourth of a mile of the association's grounds, with a penalty of a fifty-dollar fine and imprisonment until paid. Policemen appointed by the association are empowered to arrest individuals within or near the grounds for violating this liquor provision or for riotous/improper conduct, with subsequent trial or bond set by a court.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns regulations for a fairground association, specifically regarding the sale of alcohol and the powers of its police. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or segregate based on race. The penalties and enforcement mechanisms apply to all individuals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act directs the Auditor of Public Accounts to issue a warrant to pay for the support of Mildred Broadus, a pauper idiot of Lincoln County, who was found to be an idiot by the Lincoln circuit court. classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Thomas M. Purnell.
classification.summary: This act directs the Auditor of Public Accounts to pay Thomas M. Purnell, a constable, $120.60 for expenses incurred in summoning a posse to quell a riot between Irishmen and negroes in Bourbon county in October 1870. The claim had been allowed by the Bourbon circuit court, but the Auditor doubted his authority to pay it in the presented form.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Irishmen on one side and negroes on the other' in the context of a riot that required the summoning of a posse. While the act itself is about reimbursing a constable for expenses related to quelling this riot, the description of the riot's participants clearly indicates a racial dimension. However, the statute does not enact any discriminatory policies or create separate facilities. It is an administrative act to compensate an officer for services rendered during a racially charged incident. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law, but it does contain an explicit racial indicator in its description of the event.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the sale of intoxicating liquors at Cropper's Depot, in Shelby county.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale of intoxicating liquors at Cropper's Depot in Shelby County, or within one and a half miles thereof. It makes it unlawful for the county court to grant licenses for such sales. Penalties are prescribed for selling liquors, and the act is to be submitted to a vote of the legally qualified white voters residing in the specified area for ratification.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the act shall take effect whenever it shall be ratified by a majority of the 'legally qualified white voters' residing at the depot and within the specified radius. This explicit mention of 'white voters' indicates a racial restriction in the voting process for the ratification of this local option law. While the law itself is about liquor sales and not directly about racial segregation in the typical Jim Crow sense (like separate facilities or marriage restrictions), the mechanism for its implementation is racially exclusive. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow due to the explicit racial qualifier in the voting process. The confidence is high because the racial indicator is explicit. classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the leasing of the interest of the State in the Louisville and Nashville Turnpike Road, on the north side of Barren river, in the county of Warren.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Sinking Fund Commissioners of Kentucky to lease the state's interest in a specific section of the Louisville and Nashville Turnpike Road for twenty-five years. The lease is to be conducted via public auction, with the highest bidder awarded the contract. The lessee will have the rights and franchises of the state under the original charter, including the right to erect toll gates and charge tolls once the road is in good condition. The lessee is explicitly exempted from the requirement to maintain the adjacent dirt road and is entitled to collect tolls until their repair and maintenance expenditures are recouped.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the leasing of a turnpike road and the collection of tolls. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about infrastructure and finance, with no racial implications.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Central West Kentucky Agricultural and Mechanical Association. It modifies the quorum requirements for the association's board and grants the association the right to license shows, menageries, circuses, or theatrical performances on its grounds, exempting them from state taxation and licensing fees if they have a license from the association's board. It also grants the association the power to appoint and remove a chief of police and assistants to enforce its rules and preserve peace during fairs, with these police officers having powers similar to sheriffs and constables.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the town of Pryorsburg, in Graves county.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Pryorsburg in Graves County, Kentucky. It establishes a municipal government led by three trustees elected annually. The act outlines the election process for trustees, police judge, and town marshal, defines the town's boundaries, and grants the trustees the power to levy taxes and manage town finances. It also details the powers, duties, and responsibilities of the police judge and town marshal, equating them to justices of the peace and constables.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate a town and establish its municipal government. It details the election of trustees, police judge, and town marshal, defines boundaries, and grants taxing powers. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT providing for transcribing the plats, surveys, and certificates of land lying in Magoffin county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Magoffin County Court of Claims to appoint a commissioner to transcribe and record all original plats, surveys, and certificates of land lying in Magoffin County into a well-bound book. The commissioner must take an oath to accurately record these documents, which will then have legal evidentiary value. The commissioner will be paid a fee for each record, to be paid from the county levy. The completed book will be given to the county court clerk to preserve as part of the office's archives, and the clerk will be responsible for recording subsequent surveys.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative process of transcribing and recording land records in Magoffin County. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to empower the county court of Mercer county to make subscriptions to capital stock in turnpike roads in Mercer county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Mercer County, with a majority of justices concurring, to subscribe to the capital stock of turnpike road companies for roads constructed within the county. The subscriptions are limited to fifteen hundred dollars per mile. Funds will only be paid upon completion of each mile or when the court is satisfied the company can complete the road. The county court is empowered to levy an ad valorem tax, not exceeding fifty cents per hundred dollars, on taxable property to raise funds for these subscriptions, with the sheriff collecting the tax.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details an act empowering the county court of Mercer County to invest in turnpike roads. classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend and make into one the several acts relating to the charter and amendments thereto of the town of Mt. Sterling," approved March 18,1871,and torepealchapter 262.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning the town of Mt. Sterling. It authorizes the town's board of council to set a tax for tavern and liquor retailer licenses up to $500 annually. It also requires the town marshal to settle taxes and revenue quarterly and allows for compensation for ex-officio services up to $1000 annually. Additionally, it modifies the qualifications for voters in town elections to be male citizens over 21, bona fide residents for 12 months, and who have paid their capitation tax for the preceding year.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal governance of Mt. Sterling, Kentucky, specifically regarding licensing, taxation, and voter qualifications. While it sets specific requirements for voters (age, residency, tax payment), there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The voter qualifications are standard for the time and do not appear to be designed to disenfranchise any racial group. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporatethe American IndustrialCollege.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the American Industrial College for the Education of Young Men, establishing its corporators, name, and powers. It grants the college perpetual succession and the ability to contract, sue, and be sued. The college is empowered to have a common seal and adopt by-laws, rules, and regulations for its government and management, provided they are not repugnant to the laws and Constitution of the United States and Kentucky. The control and discipline of the college are to remain under Prof. Jos. B. Walker as president for at least twenty years.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the education of the children of Butler county,' approved March 10, 1871, and to levy a tax for school purposes.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning education in Butler County. It proposes a fifteen-cent school tax, but explicitly states that no taxes will be levied upon the property of any except white citizens or residents of the district. Furthermore, only white persons residing in the district and qualified to vote are entitled to vote on the adoption or rejection of this act. The tax collection and enforcement mechanisms are to be the same as for county levy and revenue taxes.

classification.title: AN ACT to require attachments to be noted of record in the office of the county clerk of Jefferson county.
classification.summary: This act mandates that any officer receiving an attachment to be levied on real estate in Jefferson County must prepare a written statement detailing the attachment's receipt date and time, the court where proceedings are pending, the names of the defendant parties and suit number, and the amount for which the attachment is issued. This statement must be delivered to the Jefferson County Clerk, who is required to index and record it within twenty-four hours in a dedicated book titled 'Memoranda of Attachments on Real Estate'. The original statement is to be filed as part of the court records, and an attested copy can be used as evidence. The act also specifies fees for the officer and the clerk for these services.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the procedural requirements for recording attachments on real estate in Jefferson County. It outlines the information that must be provided by an officer to the county clerk and the subsequent actions the clerk must take to record and index this information. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the establishment and regulation of public schools in towns.
classification.summary: This act outlines the powers and responsibilities of a board of education in managing public schools. It details provisions for appointing teachers, fixing salaries, dismissing teachers for misconduct or inefficiency, prescribing the curriculum, and determining admission rules for pupils. The act also addresses the provision of class-books for indigent children and prohibits the teaching of religious doctrines or the use of class-books that reflect upon any religious denomination. It also specifies rules regarding board member eligibility and conflicts of interest.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Incorporate the Town of Catlettsburg, in Boyd County," Approved March 5, 1872
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the incorporation of Catlettsburg. It establishes a tax for the payment of bonds, but explicitly exempts property belonging to persons of the African race from this tax. It also mandates that assessors distinguish property belonging to individuals of the African race. The act further details the use of common school funds and reporting requirements for the board of education.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that 'no property belonging to persons of the African race shall be liable to the tax provided for in this section'. This is a clear instance of racial discrimination embedded within the law, specifically concerning taxation for local improvements, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow-era legislation. The requirement for assessors to distinguish property of the 'African race' further solidifies its classification as Jim Crow. The context of the statute being an amendment to an act concerning a town's incorporation and its mention of common school funds places it within the realm of local governance and education, which were often sites of racial segregation and discrimination during this period. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year (1873).

classification.title: AN ACT repealing an act in regard to common schools in Carrollton, in Carroll county.
classification.summary: This act repeals a previous act concerning common schools in Carrollton, Carroll County. The repeal is based on a voter referendum where a majority voted against the provisions of the prior act. The general state law regarding common schools will now apply to Carrollton.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act from the Laws of Kentucky in 1873. It concerns the repeal of a local act regarding common schools in Carrollton, Carroll County, due to a voter referendum. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clear and unambiguous regarding its non-discriminatory nature.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to prohibitthe saleof spirituousand intoxicatingliquorsin Rockcastlecounty,approved March the 1st,1870 .
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regarding the sale of spirituous and intoxicating liquors in Rockcastle County. It allows the sale of ale, beer, and cider in moderate quantities, but prohibits the sale or mixing of alcoholic or spirituous liquors with these beverages. It also penalizes those who keep disorderly houses while availing themselves of this act.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation of alcohol sales in Rockcastle County, Kentucky. It amends a prior act to allow the sale of ale, beer, and cider under certain conditions, while still prohibiting the sale of spirituous liquors. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would suggest a racial discriminatory purpose. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT in relation to granting license for the sale of liquors in the town of Harrodsburg.
classification.summary: This act allows the board of trustees of Harrodsburg and the county court of Mercer county to grant licenses for the sale of spirituous or vinous liquors. It requires applicants to provide a bond ensuring they will not sell to inebriates or minors, nor on the Sabbath or election days. It also mandates payment of state tax and execution of a bond similar to existing liquor sale licenses.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales licenses in Harrodsburg. It outlines the process for obtaining a license, the conditions that must be met (e.g., not selling to minors or on specific days), and penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the sale of spirituous or vinous liquors in Nicholas county.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the county court of Nicholas County, or any person or court, from granting a license to sell spirituous or vinous liquors within the county. It also makes it unlawful for tavern-keepers, merchants, or distillers to sell such liquors in quantities less than ten gallons, except for medical use upon a physician's written certificate. Physicians are restricted from granting certificates for beverage use and must take an oath before the county court to conform to the act's provisions. Violators face a fine of one hundred dollars per offense, with fines inuring to the benefit of the county's school fund.
classification.reasoning: The statute prohibits the sale of spirituous or vinous liquors in Nicholas County, with specific regulations for medical use. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates a prohibition on liquor sales without any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend and reduce into one the several acts concerning the town of Madisonville," approved February 13th, 1873.
classification.summary: This act amends several sections of a previous act concerning the town of Madisonville. It modifies the election schedules and terms of office for the police judge and marshal, and clarifies the jurisdiction of the police judge and police court to be concurrent with justices of the peace within the town limits. It also restricts the powers and duties of the marshal in civil cases.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and electoral procedures for town officials in Madisonville, Kentucky. It details the election of a police judge and a marshal, their terms of office, and the jurisdiction of the police court. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to impose additional tax on dogs in Bracken county.
classification.summary: This act imposes a tax on dogs in Bracken County, with specific provisions for tax rates based on the number of dogs owned by housekeepers and non-housekeepers. Crucially, it mandates that the assessor keep separate lists of dogs owned by white persons and colored persons, and the sheriff is required to report the amounts collected from each racial group to the common school commissioner, who then distributes the funds equally among school districts. This explicit segregation of tax collection and reporting based on race, with the funds ultimately benefiting schools, is a hallmark of Jim Crow era legislation.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly requires the assessor to maintain separate lists for white and colored persons regarding dog ownership and taxes. Furthermore, the sheriff is mandated to report the collected taxes separately for white and colored persons. This explicit racial segregation in the collection and reporting of taxes, even for something as seemingly innocuous as dog ownership, is a clear indicator of Jim Crow practices. The funds are then directed towards school districts, suggesting a racialized approach to public services. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to establish a system of common schools in the town of Corydon, in Henderson county."

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in Lincoln county, and to take a vote on the same.
classification.summary: This act makes it unlawful to sell spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in Lincoln County in quantities less than one barrel (if whisky) or ten gallons (if wine, beer, or ale). Druggists are permitted to sell liquors in small quantities upon a written prescription from a physician for medicinal purposes, provided these prescriptions are kept on file. The act requires a vote of the qualified citizens of Lincoln County to determine if these provisions will take effect.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
1873.
## CHAPTER 890.
AN ACT in relation to persons convicted of penal offenses in Union county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

Persons found guilty of penal offenses to work out fines on roads
§ 1. That it may be lawful for the judge of the Union circuit court, the presiding judge of the Union county court, or the police judge of the towns of Morganfield, Caseyville, or Uniontown, in Union county, to order any person or persons, who may be convicted of a penal offense in the said circuit court, or before the said presiding judge, or any justice of Union county, or before either of the said police judges, and who shall fail to pay the fine or fines imposed against him or them, to work upon the public roads of Union county, under the supervisor of public roads or any of his assistants, or any contractor for work on said roads. The said person or persons to be allowed a credit of two dollars per day for such labor, and shall be required to work long enough to pay the full amount of their fines.

Jailer to furnish lodging for such prisoners.
§ 2. The jailer of Union county, or the keeper of the station-house in said town, shall be required to furnish lodging in the jail or station-house, and to furnish sustenance for such person or persons whilst at work, and during Sundays or other intervals when not at work: *Provided*, That for every Sunday or day not employed in laboring on the roads, said person or persons shall be allowed a credit of two dollars on his or their fine.

§ 3. That this act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved April 18, 1873.

---

§ 1. That the Auditor of Public Accounts draw his warrant on the Treasury in favor of R. A. Thomason, for the sum of two hundred and seventy dollars and twenty-seven

The redemption is contingent upon payment of the sale price plus interest within five years. Upon payment, title to the property would revert to Morgan, and he would retain possession as long as he has the right to redeem.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act authorizing the trustees of school district No. 2, in Hickman county, to levy and collect a tax for school purposes
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning school district No. 2 in Hickman County. It allows the trustees to annually estimate necessary funds for school maintenance, including common school fund draws. They can levy and collect a per-head tax of one dollar from inhabitants over twenty-one and from parents/guardians of scholastic-age minors. The remaining funds needed can be levied and collected from personal and real estate within the district, as assessed for revenue and county levy purposes. The collector is granted sheriff-like powers and responsibilities. Inconsistent provisions from the original act are repealed.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the funding and administration of a specific school district. While it deals with taxation and school operations, there is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The tax is levied on inhabitants and minors based on age and property, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the retail of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in the towns of Hudsonville, Constantine, and Cross Roads, in Breckinridge county, or in any part of said county within six miles of either of said towns.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the county court and town trustees from granting licenses to sell spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors by retail in the towns of Hudsonville, Constantine, and Cross Roads, or within a six-mile radius of these towns in Breckinridge County. Exceptions are made for merchants, druggists, and distillers who can sell in quantities of not less than a quart, to be taken away and not consumed on the premises. The act also outlines penalties for selling without a license and requires a local election to approve its implementation.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales within specific geographic areas of Breckinridge County, Kentucky. It sets restrictions on who can be licensed to sell alcohol and in what quantities. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The act's focus is purely on the regulation of alcohol sales and licensing, with a provision for local voter approval. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

1873. ruary each year, and may by proper ordinances require said board to grade and value all the real estate and improvements thereon, should there be any, before the assessment is made; which graduation and valuation, if made, shall be a criterion for the assessor in making his valuation of the same. The said board shall have the same power to sit after the assessment is returned, and to pass upon the same, as is now prescribed by law. The city council shall appoint the assessor in the months of January or February each year, and require him to take the oath of office, and give bond for a faithful performance of his duties. The council shall have power to appoint assistant assessors, and shall require oath of office and bonds of them. The city council may, by ordinances, prescribe the forms and manner of assessment, and the time at which it shall be done. The council shall allow a fair compensation to the assessor, assistant assessors, and board of equalization for their services; and all laws requiring the assessor for the city to be elected by the people, are hereby repealed.

**Council to appoint assessor, who shall give bond.**

**Compensation of assessors, &c.**

§ 3. That the city council shall have power to purchase real estate suitable for a union public graded school for said city, and for the purpose of buying the same and erecting suitable buildings thereon, the council shall have power to issue and sell bonds of the city to an amount not exceeding $50,000, the bonds to be in such sums as the council shall designate, and to bear interest at not exceeding eight per cent. per annum, payable semi-annually, at such times and places as may be stipulated in the bonds and coupons; the interest on said bonds to be provided for each year by a tax levied for that purpose; and a failure and refusal on the part of council to make such provision for the payment of the interest, and finally for the principal of said bonds, shall render the individual members of said council so failing and refusing, personally responsible for the same. The bonds may run for any length of time not exceeding twenty years. The city council shall have power to sell and convey any or all of the present school property or other real estate owned by the city, and apply the proceeds thereof toward paying for the said union school property.

**Council may purchase property and establish graded schools.**

**May issue bonds for said purpose.**

**May sell city property to pay bonds.**

§ 4. That the city council shall have power to establish and govern by ordinances public schools for the colored children of said city, between the ages of six and twenty years, and to levy, collect, and set apart for that purpose the same taxes upon the persons and property of the colored people in said city as they levy and collect upon the persons and property of the white people in said city, for public schools. The council shall prescribe the number of colored trustees for such schools,

**May establish, and make regulations to govern, colored schools, levy tax on colored people, fix number of trustees, &c.**

classification.summary: This section of the laws of Kentucky from 1873 outlines provisions for colored public schools. It grants power to colored trustees to levy and collect a prorata tax from parents or guardians to cover any deficiencies in funding for teaching, beyond the amount realized from taxes levied by the council. The council is responsible for prescribing teacher qualifications and examination modes, and generally enforcing the section to ensure the efficiency of these schools. The subsequent sections deal with offenders in Maysville working on streets instead of the workhouse and the erection of station-houses, and an act to incorporate the town of Norton.

1873. or any material necessary or requisite for said roads and bridges; and the right of way and use of lands or quarries, either by agreement with the several proprietors, or by having the same condemned to the use of said company; and in the condemnation thereof for any road or railroad, and in the location, construction, and operation of such roads or railroads, with all requisite appendages, said company shall have the powers which are provided in chapter one hundred and three of the Revised Statutes, entitled "Turnpike and Plank Roads." When such roads, railroads, or bridges shall be used by other parties for travel or transportation, said company may charge and collect such rates of toll as the board of directors may determine, not exceeding the rates permitted by law to be charged by other corporations specially chartered to furnish such facilities for travel and transportation.

Capital stock.
§ 4. The capital stock of said company shall not exceed one million of dollars, to be divided into shares of one hundred dollars each, which shall be personal property, and evidenced and transferred as may be prescribed by the by-laws of said company. The persons named in this act shall constitute the first board of directors; they, or a majority of them, shall have power to open books for subscriptions to the stock of said company, prescribe the terms of subscription, the time and manner of payment, and the time, manner, and grounds of forfeiture of stock subscribed, but not paid for, as required by the calls; and they may forfeit the stock of the defaulting stockholder, or collect the installments by suit, as they may elect; they may adopt such by-laws for the regulation and government of said company as they deem expedient, not inconsistent, however, with the Constitution and laws of this State, nor inconsistent with the Constitution and laws of the United States. When the sum of one hundred thousand dollars shall be subscribed, and such portion thereof paid in cash as the directors may require, the company shall be deemed fully organized, and may proceed with its business.

General powers of directors.
§ 5. The board of directors shall have the general management of all the business of said company, and may exercise any and all the powers hereby granted to the corporation; they may increase the capital stock of the company from time to time, as they may deem expedient, either by new subscriptions, sales, or otherwise, to the extent of the limit named in section four of this act; they may, from time to time, pay dividends in stock or in cash, from the profits of their business, and may distribute the net receipts and proceeds of sales among the stockholders *pro rata*, or may retain them to be applied in extending the operations of the company; they may borrow money

classification.title: An Act to amend an act, entitled "An act to provide for the education of the children of the Commonwealth," approved March 21, 1872, so far as it relates to the county of Lawrence.
classification.summary: This act authorizes trustees in a specific school district in Lawrence County to levy taxes for school purposes. Crucially, it explicitly states that the property of negroes shall not be taxed and that no poll or capitation tax shall be assessed upon persons of African descent. Funds collected from these taxes are to be used for erecting or purchasing a schoolhouse.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly exempts 'negroes' and 'persons of African descent' from property and poll taxes for school purposes. This differential treatment based on race, even if framed as an exemption, is a characteristic of Jim Crow legislation that created separate and unequal systems. The exemption from taxation for school purposes for Black individuals, while allowing taxation for others, implies a segregated or underfunded educational system for Black children, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly in 1873. The confidence is high because the racial distinction is explicit and directly impacts the funding of education.
