CHAPTER 927.
AN ACT to prevent trespass upon inclosed lands in Harrison county.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*
Stock to be kept off of roads in parts of Harrison county.
§ 1. That all persons owning horses, hogs, sheep, or cattle, in that part of Harrison county hereinafter described, shall be required to keep the same within their own inclosure; and that any person living within said boundary, who shall suffer or knowingly permit any of his stock to stray or go at large upon the highways in said county, for twenty-four hours in succession, without a keeper or some one to look after and control them, shall be fined in any sum not less than five nor more than twenty dollars, to be recovered by warrant before a justice of the peace in and for said county.
Penalty.
§ 2. That this act shall only apply to that part of Harrison county south of the following line, to-wit: Beginning at Scott's Station; thence with the Scott Station and Cynthiana Turnpike to the White school-house; thence with the dirt road to Zion meeting-house on Cynthiana and Oddville Turnpike; thence down the same to the first left hand dirt road; thence with said road to Poindexter's old mill; thence with the dirt road to the farm of Ed. Burns, on Cynthiana and Raven Creek Turnpike; thence with said pike to Unity; thence with the usual dirt road to Connersville; thence with the Connersville and Leesburg Road to Silas creek.
Boundary of said district.
§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved April 21, 1873.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Lebanon, Kentucky. It outlines the powers and duties of the town trustees, including their ability to make by-laws, levy taxes, and collect fines. It also establishes the election of a police judge and town marshal, detailing their powers, duties, fees, and bond requirements. The marshal is given broad authority to serve processes, collect taxes and debts, and is subject to the same regulations as sheriffs and constables.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Green River Navigation Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Green River Navigation Company with a capital of fifty thousand dollars, divided into shares of twenty-five dollars each. The corporation is granted perpetual succession and the power to sue and be sued, have a common seal, and make by-laws. It is empowered to transport persons and property on Green River and other necessary rivers, own and operate watercraft, acquire and sell real estate for its purposes, and make contracts with shippers. The company's business will be managed by a board of seven directors elected annually by stockholders. Commissioners are appointed to open books and receive stock subscriptions.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the several acts concerning the management of the Wilderness Turnpike Road.
classification.summary: This act abolishes the toll gate in Pineville, Bell County, and consolidates toll collection to a single gate on the Wilderness Turnpike Road. The gate will be located in Knox County or between Flat Lick and Cumberland Ford in Bell County. The gate will be sold annually to the highest bidder, and the judge of the Knox county court is authorized to conduct the sale. Purchasers are authorized to collect tolls from all travelers except citizens of Bell and Knox counties, who are exempted. Penalties are established for those who refuse to pay or attempt to evade the toll.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management of a turnpike road, specifically the location and operation of toll gates. It exempts citizens of Bell and Knox counties from paying tolls. While this creates a geographical exemption, there is no indication within the text that this exemption is based on race. The statute does not mention race explicitly or implicitly in its provisions. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the settlement of the affairs of the road in Bell, Knox, and Laurel counties, and for the sale of the toll-gate on the Crab Orchard branch of said road.
classification.summary: This act mandates county courts in Bell, Knox, and Laurel counties to appoint commissioners to settle the financial affairs of a specific road. These commissioners are tasked with investigating past and present county judges and gatekeepers regarding road funds, identifying road indebtedness, and reporting their findings to the county courts. The act also authorizes the sale of a toll-gate on the Crab Orchard branch of the road, specifying the process for sale and the location of the new toll collection point. Debts are to be paid according to their recorded priority.

The focus is purely on administrative and financial matters related to road infrastructure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to charter the Ohio River, Owenton, and Lexington Railway Company.
classification.summary: This act charters the Ohio River, Owenton, and Lexington Railway Company, establishing it as a body politic and corporate. It outlines the company's name, perpetual succession, powers, and the roles of appointed commissioners to receive subscriptions for its capital stock. The capital stock is set at a maximum of two million dollars, divided into shares of one hundred dollars each, and can be owned by individuals, counties, towns, cities, and other corporations on an equal footing. The company can organize once fifty thousand dollars in stock subscriptions are made.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to charter a railway company. It details the establishment, powers, and capital structure of the corporation. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also establishes procedures for notifying owners and for jury trials to determine compensation.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate The Rockcastle Railway Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates "The Rockcastle Railway Company" with a capital stock of five hundred thousand dollars, divided into shares of one hundred dollars each. It outlines the company's ability to contract, sue and be sued, and use a common seal. The act details procedures for opening books for stock subscriptions, electing a president and seven directors, and making by-laws. It also specifies that meetings can be held within the state with proper notice, and an office and agent must be maintained in Lawrence county for service of process. Actions against the company can be brought in any county where any part of the railway lies, with exceptions for actions mentioned in sections ninety-three and ninety-four of the Code of Practice in civil cases.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of school district No. 17, in Garrard county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the trustees of school district No. 17 in Garrard County to levy a tax on taxable property listed by white persons to fund the construction of a schoolhouse. The tax is not to exceed fifty cents on the hundred dollars annually, and the total collected should not exceed seven hundred dollars. All property listed for state revenue within the district is subject to distraint for this tax. The sheriff is to collect the tax and pay it to the trustees, retaining a commission. The trustees must execute a bond for their duties.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to prohibit the sale of intoxicating liquors in the county of Bullitt, approved March 21st, 1871.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law prohibiting the sale of intoxicating liquors in Bullitt County. It requires individuals furnishing liquors for medical purposes, based on a physician's written prescription, to record a copy of the prescription and report it under oath to the trustee of the jury fund. Failure to comply is deemed as keeping a tippling-house. The act also states that selling liquor under a written prescription is not a defense to a prosecution under this act.

classification.reasoning: The provided statute, Chapter 991 of the Laws of Kentucky from 1873, deals with the jurisdiction of courts in lunacy inquests in Jefferson County and the city of Louisville. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and unrelated to racial matters.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of common school district No. 34, in Bracken county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Superintendent of Public Instruction to certify the sixty percentum due to common school district No. 34 in Bracken county for the year ending June 30, 1873. The Auditor is to draw a warrant on the Treasurer in favor of the commissioner for payment. The commissioner is to collect and pay this amount, along with the forty percentum already in their hands, to aid the common school in the district. The commissioner is granted an extension until June 30, 1874, to report the school to the Superintendent. The act takes effect upon passage.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act for the benefit of a specific common school district in Bracken county, Kentucky. It deals with the financial allocation and reporting procedures for that district's school funds. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly administrative and financial, with no racial implications.

It also specifies provisions for capital stock, bond issuance, and securing these bonds through mortgages or deeds of trust.

It focuses on business activities, financial structures (capital stock, bonds), and property management. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company.
classification.summary: This act details financial and operational aspects of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, including procedures for foreclosing on mortgages or deeds of trust, establishing a sinking fund for bond redemption, and granting the company the right to connect its manufacturing establishment with railroads within Louisville with the consent of the city's general council. It specifies how foreclosure proceeds benefit bondholders and how the sinking fund is to be managed and used solely for bond redemption. It also allows for the company to use its own bonds for retiring debt before maturity.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the financial and operational regulations of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company. It discusses mortgage foreclosures, bond redemption through a sinking fund, and the company's ability to connect to railroads within Louisville. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the organization of the public schools in the town of Clayville, in Webster county.
classification.summary: This act consolidates three common school districts (Nos. 17, 45, and 46) in Webster County into a single district named Clayville District. It outlines the process for ratifying this consolidation through a popular vote, establishes a board of education with elected members, and grants this board control over the district's public schools, property, and funds. The act also details the qualifications for voters in the election of the board and the powers of the board, including making by-laws and rules for school governance.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the organization and management of public schools in Clayville, Kentucky. It details the consolidation of school districts, the election of a board of trustees, and the powers of that board. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on the administrative and organizational aspects of the local school system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the common school laws of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act outlines the powers and duties of a board of trustees for common schools. Crucially, Section 10 explicitly states that only white children are to be admitted to these schools, and Section 14 allows the board to levy taxes for school purposes, exempting property owned by colored persons from taxation for school revenue. This demonstrates a clear racial segregation in the provision of public education and a discriminatory tax policy.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states in Section 10 that 'only white children be admitted to said schools.' This is a direct and unambiguous racial exclusion from public education. Furthermore, Section 14 exempts property owned by 'colored persons' from taxation for school revenue, indicating a discriminatory financial policy tied to race. These provisions clearly align with the characteristics of Jim Crow laws, specifically in the realm of education. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1873'.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the education of the children of the Commonwealth'
classification.summary: This section of the statute outlines the process for establishing and funding schools within a district. It details the powers of the board of trustees, including purchasing property, furnishing schools, and paying teachers. Crucially, it mandates that tax lists for school purposes in July 1873 and each succeeding year must be made out by listing "all the white owners of property in said district, and of the amount and value of property owned by each subject to taxation for revenue purposes, and of all the white tithes therein." This explicit racial classification for taxation purposes in the context of education funding is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white owners of property' and 'white tithes' when detailing the creation of tax lists for school purposes. This direct racial classification for the purpose of taxation and funding of schools, especially in the context of the post-Reconstruction era, strongly indicates a Jim Crow law. The law is related to education, and the document type is session laws based on the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 507 ! - 1873 .'.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the town of Brandenburg, and to provide for the establishment of a graded school therein.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the board of trustees of the graded school in Brandenburg to levy taxes, issue bonds for school buildings and grounds, and admit non-resident or out-of-age white children upon payment of charges. It also states that common school laws not inconsistent with this act remain in force and that the school is entitled to its portion of the public school fund.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' in Section 20, indicating a racial classification within the educational system. While it doesn't establish segregation in the same way as other Jim Crow laws, the explicit mention of race in the context of school admission makes it relevant to a Jim Crow archive. However, it does not appear to be a core Jim Crow law mandating segregation or disenfranchisement, but rather a provision that reflects the racial norms of the time. The confidence is high because the racial indicator is explicit. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V2-1873.pdf' and the year 1873, which corresponds to a legislative session.

classification.title: AN ACT to establish schools for the education of children of citizens of African descent in McCracken county
classification.summary: This act establishes a tax fund specifically for the education of "colored children" in McCracken County. It mandates an annual tax of twenty cents on each one hundred dollars' worth of property owned by persons of color and a poll tax of one dollar on each colored male resident over eighteen years of age.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'children of citizens of African descent' and 'colored children' and 'persons of color', clearly indicating a racial basis for the law. The law establishes a separate funding mechanism for the education of Black children, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow-era segregation in education. The confidence is high because of the explicit racial language and the nature of the law (separate educational funding). The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly. The category is 'education' due to the explicit purpose of the act.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the establishment and support of colored schools in the city of Paducah and county of McCracken.
classification.summary: This act mandates the establishment of separate school districts for colored children in Paducah and McCracken County. It outlines the duties of the common school commissioner in laying out these districts, conducting elections for trustees, and overseeing the schools. Crucially, it specifies that teachers and trustees must be colored people, and that funds will be distributed proportionally to colored schools. The act also details the collection of taxes for these schools and the reporting requirements for the commissioner.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates the creation of separate school districts for 'colored schools' and requires that teachers and trustees for these schools be 'colored people'. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The act's focus on establishing and funding separate educational facilities for Black children, while distinct from white schools, firmly places it within the context of Jim Crow legislation. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year (1873) and is part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY'.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate official advertisements of sales of sheriffs and master commissioners in the county of Jessamine.
classification.summary: This act specifies the requirements for official advertisements of sales of property by sheriffs and master commissioners in Jessamine County. It mandates that sales exceeding three hundred dollars must be advertised in two consecutive newspaper issues within the county, with the cost borne by the seller. The act also caps the advertising cost at twelve dollars per advertisement.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Tradewater River Improvement Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Tradewater River Improvement Company, granting it powers to improve navigation on the Tradewater River and its tributaries by constructing locks and dams, creating water power for manufacturing, and building roads and railways. The company can also cut and manufacture timber, operate mining and manufacturing machinery, and use boats and barges. It is authorized to collect tolls and to condemn lands necessary for its improvements. The statute specifies that these powers should not obstruct river use by other parties beyond what is necessary for the improvements, nor cause overflow of lands not owned by the company.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a company for river improvements and grants it various business powers. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to charter the public schools of Falmouth and district No. 1, in Pendleton county.
classification.summary: This act charters the establishment, support, and maintenance of public schools in Falmouth and an adjacent district. It specifies that a board of three trustees, elected annually by qualified white voters, will control the schools. The trustees are empowered to provide schoolhouses and schools for white children and are created as a corporate body with perpetual succession. The property and funds of the schools are dedicated to their purpose and are exempt from debt and taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'qualified white voters' for electing trustees and 'white children' as the beneficiaries of the public schools. This direct racial distinction in the provision of public education is a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with explicit racial indicators in the education category. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly in 1873.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact an Act entitled "An Act to Incorporate the Trustees of the Common School District No. 1, in the County of Pendleton, and to provide for the support and management of said school."
classification.summary: This act details the powers and responsibilities of a school board in District No. 1, Pendleton County, Kentucky. It outlines their authority to employ and pay teachers, manage school finances, appoint officers, and organize the school into departments. Notably, it specifies that the high school department requires a tuition fee and cannot be funded by common school funds or local property/capitation taxes. The act also allows for the transfer of property from existing school trustees to the newly created board.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the organization and funding of a school district. While it mentions the establishment of a high school department with tuition fees, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or racial segregation. The provisions are administrative and financial in nature, related to the management of a local school. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 1014.
AN ACT for the benefit of common school district No. 1, in the county of Jessamine.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

Nicholasville may establish graded schools & studies.
§ 1. That in addition to primary school provided for by an act to revise, amend, and to reduce into one the laws relating to common schools in Kentucky, approved March 21st, 1870; also an act to amend an act to revise, amend, and to reduce into one the laws relating to common schools of Kentucky, approved March 13th, 1871; also an act to allow common school districts to levy a district school tax, approved March 2d, 1865; and also an act for the benefit of common schools in Jessamine county, approved February 28th, 1873, the trustees of district No. 1, Jessamine county, embracing the town of Nicholasville, may establish graded schools, constituted of primary, intermediate, or high school departments; the primary school, as provided for as above, shall be the primary department; the intermediate department shall embrace a more advanced course of study, which shall include mental and practical arithmetic, geography, English grammar, composition, penmanship and book-keeping, history, and Constitution of the United States, besides such other branches as trustees or the State Board of Education may recommend or select. The high school department shall embrace the highest course of studies prescribed and allowed in the public free schools, and shall include natural philosophy, physiology, algebra, geometry, rhetoric, logic, mental and moral philosophy, besides such other branches as may be selected as above.

Boundary.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
1873.
## CHAPTER 1028.
AN ACT to pay the expenses of D. R. Haggard, State agent sent to Missouri after Cutler and Taylor, charged with forgery.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the Auditor of Public Accounts be, and he is, directed to draw his warrant upon the treasury for two hundred and eighty-three dollars, in favor of D. R. Haggard, for money expended by him in the pursuit of Norman Cutler and John M. Taylor, of Missouri, charged with the crime of forgery.
§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.
Approved April 23, 1873

---

## CHAPTER 1030.
AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend the charter of the Elizabethtown and Paducah Railroad Company," approved February 28, 1873.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That nothing in the act, entitled "An act to amend the charter of the Elizabethtown and Paducah Railroad Company," approved February 28, 1873, shall be so construed as to prevent the construction of the branch road from Elizabethtown to Louisville.
§ 2. That this act shall take effect from its passage.
Approved April 23, 1873

---

## CHAPTER 1031.
AN ACT for the benefit of school district No. 21, in Carroll county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the trustees of school district No. 21, in Carroll county, are hereby authorized and empowered to make their reports of the school taught in said district for the school year ending June 30th, 1873, without swearing to the same; and that said report shall be received by the school commissioner for said county, and shall, in all respects, have the same effect, and be acted on by him in the same manner, as though it had been sworn to by the trustees of said district.
§ 2. That this act shall take effect from its passage.
Approved April 23, 1873

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the education of colored children in Garrard county.
classification.summary: This act directs the county commissioner of common schools in Garrard county to lay off the county into suitable districts for colored children, ensuring no district has more than one hundred or less than twenty colored children of pupilage. It also mandates the election of three colored trustees by qualified colored voters in each district, who will be responsible for providing school-houses, hiring teachers, and notifying parents of the free educational opportunity for their children. The trustees will also report the length of the school term to the commissioner. The act grants trustees the power to purchase land for school-houses.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored children' and establishes separate school districts and elected trustees for them. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The act's purpose is to provide education specifically for 'colored children,' implying a separate and distinct system from that of white children, even though the latter is not explicitly mentioned in this particular act. The confidence is high because of the explicit racial designation and the establishment of separate educational structures. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly in a specific year.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the education of colored children in the county of [County Name]
classification.summary: This act establishes a separate system for the education of colored children within a specific county. It mandates a tax on colored persons for school purposes, including the erection of school-houses and payment of qualified teachers. It also specifies that new school-houses for colored children cannot be located too close to existing school-houses for white children, with exceptions for towns. The act outlines the roles of the county commissioner and sheriff in collecting and disbursing these funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored persons' and 'white children' in the context of education, establishing separate provisions and restrictions for each group. The requirement for separate school-houses and the specific distance regulations between them for white and colored children are clear indicators of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The taxation and disbursement mechanisms are also specifically tied to the 'colored persons' within the county, further solidifying its classification as a Jim Crow law.

If owners fail to comply, the city can perform the work at the owner's expense, with the cost becoming a lien on the property. The act also allows the city to abate other nuisances on private property at the owner's cost and impose fines.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 575
classification.summary: This statute details the election and terms of office for school trustees in specific wards of a city. It outlines how terms are staggered, how vacancies are filled, and the duties of the city clerk as ex-officio clerk of the board. It also grants the council power to enact ordinances to suppress certain establishments (tippling-houses, bawdy-houses, gambling-houses, etc.) and to regulate the retail of liquors, as well as to abate nuisances on private property. The penalties for violations are to be set by ordinance.
classification.reasoning: The statute primarily concerns the administrative and regulatory functions of a city government, specifically regarding school trustees, public order, and property nuisances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted to the council, such as suppressing 'bawdy-houses' and regulating liquor sales, are general in nature and do not appear to be racially motivated based on the text provided. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Ludlow
classification.summary: This act grants the city council of Ludlow exclusive rights to license and regulate taverns, houses of public entertainment, coffee-houses, retailers of liquors, and houses of public resort. It also outlines provisions for the city to borrow money, issue bonds, and handle the temporary disability or absence of the mayor. Additionally, it empowers the council to improve streets and alleys at the expense of lot owners. A key provision prohibits the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors on Sundays or general election days, with penalties for violations.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal governance of Ludlow, Kentucky, specifically concerning licensing, borrowing, and public works. While it includes regulations on the sale of alcoholic beverages, particularly prohibiting sales on Sundays and election days, there is no mention or implication of racial discrimination. The language used is general and applies to all individuals and businesses within the city. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville, and to Authorize the Grading and Paving of Streets, Alleys, Market Spaces, and Lanes, and the Curbing and Paving of Sidewalks.
classification.summary: This act grants the city council of Louisville the power to order and contract for the grading, paving, curbing, macadamizing, bowldering, or turnpiking of streets, alleys, market spaces, and lanes. It outlines the process for initiating such improvements, including requiring petitions from property owners or unanimous council consent. The act also details the apportionment of costs among lot owners, the establishment of liens on properties, and redemption periods for sold lots, with different terms for those who consented to the improvements versus those who did not, and special provisions for infants, femes covert, and persons of unsound mind. Additionally, it specifically empowers the council to curb and pave sidewalks and apportion costs among bordering lot owners.
classification.reasoning: The statute details procedures for municipal improvements such as street paving and sidewalk construction within the city of Louisville. It outlines the process for initiating these improvements, financing them through property owner assessments, and establishing liens. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race or racial segregation. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the legislature in a specific year (1873). The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on infrastructure and municipal finance without any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the manufacturing, storing, keeping, and transporting nitro-glycerine, in Mason county.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the manufacturing, storing, or keeping of nitro-glycerine within one mile of any city, town, or dwelling in Mason County, unless separated by a mountain or hill of at least two hundred feet in height and at least one-fourth of a mile away. It also mandates that the substance be stored in a congealed condition, not transported on passenger conveyances, and transported in accordance with federal laws. Owners are liable for damages and manslaughter in case of explosions. The circuit court of Mason County has jurisdiction over violations.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of a dangerous substance (nitro-glycerine) and its storage and transportation within Mason County. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would disproportionately affect a specific racial group. The regulations are based on public safety concerns related to the explosive nature of the substance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of William Tinsley, late sheriff of Knox county.
classification.summary: This act grants William Tinsley, former sheriff of Knox county, an additional two years from April 1, 1873, to collect his fee bills and uncollected taxes. It allows him to list these with the current sheriff or any constable of Knox county for collection, subject to existing laws regarding illegal distraining and collecting.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 47 of the General Statutes, Relating to Deeds of Trust
classification.summary: This act outlines the procedures for trustees in executing deeds of trust. It details the process for selling property conveyed in a deed of trust when the grantor defaults on payments, including notice requirements, terms of sale, and the trustee's duty to execute a deed to the purchaser. It also specifies the trustee's actions in case of a purchaser's default, including reselling the property and holding the original purchaser liable for any difference in price. The act further details how the proceeds of sale should be applied, including expenses, debts, and any surplus to the grantor. Finally, it addresses the appointment of a new trustee in case of the original trustee's death.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for executing deeds of trust and handling property sales in cases of default. It focuses on the rights and responsibilities of grantors, trustees, and purchasers. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Cincinnati Southern Railroad
classification.summary: This act grants the Cincinnati Southern Railroad company the right to locate and build a railway and bridge across the Ohio River. It outlines procedures for public notification of site selection, acquisition of land and materials through eminent domain, and compensation for landowners. The act specifies that the trustees of the railroad have the right to claim and take possession of the selected sites after providing notice and compensating the company for expenditures with interest. It also details the process for appointing commissioners to assess compensation when agreements cannot be reached with landowners, particularly in cases involving non-residents, femes covert, or individuals of unsound mind.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and operational rights of the Cincinnati Southern Railroad, specifically focusing on land acquisition and construction of a railway and bridge. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The procedures described for land acquisition and compensation are standard for infrastructure projects and do not appear to have a racial dimension. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: INDEX
classification.summary: This entry is an index to the Acts of the Kentucky General Assembly for 1873. It lists various acts by subject and the page number where they can be found. The specific entries include acts for the benefit of individuals, acts related to specific towns and companies (e.g., Winchester, Winchester Cemetery Company), acts concerning roads (e.g., Winchester and Muddy Creek Turnpike Road Company), acts related to courts (e.g., Wolfe circuit court), and acts concerning waterways (e.g., Wolf creek).
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an index to legislative acts from 1873. It does not contain the text of any specific statute, but rather a list of titles and page numbers for various acts. None of the listed act titles suggest any racial component or connection to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with full confidence. The category is null as it is not a statute but an index. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to acts passed by the General Assembly. There is no racial indicator.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment of property for taxation, and for the collection of taxes therein mentioned," approved March 10, 1871.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous tax assessment and collection law. It details procedures for the assessment of property and the collection of taxes, including provisions for delinquent taxes and the duties of tax collectors. It does not appear to contain any racial provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to a tax law. Upon review of the text, there are no explicit or implicit mentions of race, nor are there any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory racial purpose. The focus is solely on the administrative and procedural aspects of property taxation and tax collection. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.
